title: Oxidized Oil on Cotton — Alkaline Saponification & Polymer Breaking
stainChemistry: Oxidized or rancid cooking oil that has been exposed to air for extended time undergoes polymerization and cross-linking. The triglyceride fatty acids (the main component of cooking oil) oxidize through free-radical reactions, forming hydroperoxides and then stable cross-linked polymers. This polymerized mass is fundamentally different from fresh oil — it's a thermoset polymer, not a fluid triglyceride. Standard nonionic detergent (NSD) or perchloroethylene (POG treatment) will FAIL on oxidized oil because standard spotting assumes fluid grease that can be emulsified. Oxidized oil is a solid, cross-linked polymer mass.
whyThisWorks: Oxidized oil polymerization can be partially reversed through strong alkaline treatment, which causes saponification (breaking of ester bonds) and hydrolysis of the cross-linked polymer network. Potassium hydroxide (KOH) solution is used to break apart the polymer chain cross-links and convert any remaining fatty acid esters to soluble soap (potassium salts of fatty acids). This converts the insoluble polymer mass back into soluble or emulsifiable form. Following with a moderate oxidizing treatment can complete removal of polymerized residues.

spottingProtocol.agent: sodium or potassium hydroxide (KOH) powder — very light dusting
spottingProtocol.technique: dry powder application with absolute minimal moisture
spottingProtocol.equipment: spotting brush, white cotton terry pad, soft brush, glass rod
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply a VERY LIGHT dusting of potassium hydroxide powder directly to the oxidized oil stain. Do NOT wet the area yet — work dry. Use only a trace amount — too much KOH will cause uneven damage to cotton fibers. Work it gently into the stain with a glass rod or ceramic brush (not metal). This preliminary step begins breaking polymer cross-links. After 2-3 minutes, brush off the excess powder gently.

spottingProtocol.agent: potassium hydroxide (KOH) solution (1% KOH in water — 1 oz lye per 100 oz water) EXTREME CAUTION
spottingProtocol.technique: gentle sponging with careful saturation control
spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, glass or ceramic container, wooden or glass rod, pH paper
spottingProtocol.instruction: Prepare KOH solution very carefully in a glass or ceramic container. NEVER use metal — KOH reacts with metal. Sponge the dilute KOH solution very gently into the oxidized oil stain from the back side. Work slowly and carefully — KOH is caustic and can damage cotton if concentration is too high. Keep the area moderately moist (not soaking). As you work, the dark polymerized oil will gradually soften and become lighter. This step saponifies and hydrolyzes the polymer network. Use pH paper to monitor — once pH reaches 9-10, move to next step.

spottingProtocol.agent: distilled water (rinse to neutral pH — essential step)
spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, spotting bottle, pH paper
spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse EXTENSIVELY with distilled water to remove all KOH residue. This is absolutely critical — excess KOH on cotton causes yellowing and fiber damage. Use pH paper to verify neutrality. Perform multiple rinses until pH paper shows neutral pH (around 7). This is non-negotiable.

spottingProtocol.agent: hydrogen peroxide 3% (optional — only if brown residue remains)
spottingProtocol.instruction: If oxidized oil residue remains as brown or tan color after KOH saponification, apply hydrogen peroxide 3% to oxidize and bleach remaining polymer fragments. Work gently. This is a secondary step — many oxidized oil stains will respond fully to KOH treatment alone.

spottingProtocol.agent: 5% acetic acid solution (white vinegar 1:3 with water)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Final sponge with acetic acid to fully neutralize any remaining alkaline residue and restore fiber pH. This prevents post-spotting yellowing.

escalation: If oxidized oil stain persists after the full KOH protocol (saponification + rinsing + oxidation), the polymer may be extremely cross-linked or the stain may be old and partially fixed to the fiber. Do not repeat KOH treatment — this will damage cotton. Professional treatment with heated alkali (done in controlled conditions) or xylene (a stronger solvent) is the only remaining option, but carries significant fiber damage risk. Very old oxidized oil stains are often permanent.
commonMistakes: Using standard nonionic detergent or perchloroethylene on oxidized oil — these will NOT work because the oil is polymerized, not fluid.
Over-concentration of KOH — high concentration will damage cotton fibers before dissolving the stain.
Not rinsing thoroughly after KOH — alkali residue causes yellowing.
Using hot water or heat — heat accelerates polymer re-oxidation and can set the stain deeper.
scienceNote: The distinction between fresh oil and oxidized oil is absolutely critical in professional spotting. A spotter using standard POG treatment on an oxidized oil stain is like using gasoline to clean rust — wrong tool for the job. Oxidized oil is a synthetic polymer formed through free-radical chain reactions and cross-linking. Only strong alkaline treatment (saponification and ester hydrolysis) can break this polymer network. Understanding oxidation chemistry and polymer cross-linking is what separates professional spotters from generalists.
safety: CRITICAL HAZARD: Potassium hydroxide (KOH, also called caustic potash or lye) is HIGHLY CAUSTIC. It causes severe chemical burns. ALWAYS wear heavy nitrile gloves and eye protection. Work in a well-ventilated area. In case of contact with skin, flush immediately with water for at least 15 minutes and seek medical attention. Do NOT use metal containers — KOH reacts with metal. Use only glass or ceramic containers. Keep away from children and pets. Hydrogen peroxide 3% is safe. Acetic acid (vinegar) is safe.
materialWarnings: Cotton is resistant to KOH at dilute concentrations (0.5-1%) but can be damaged at higher concentrations.
NEVER use strong KOH on cotton — it will cause severe fiber weakening and yellowing.
After KOH treatment, cotton is more fragile and sensitive to heat — air dry only, no heat.
Test all chemicals on a hidden seam first.
Some specialty cotton finishes may be affected by strong alkali.
callPro: For valuable cotton garments, STRONGLY RECOMMENDED to use professional treatment. KOH is hazardous and requires careful handling. Professionals have proper safety equipment and can control concentrations carefully. DIY KOH spotting carries serious injury risk (chemical burns) and fiber damage risk.
customerExplanation: Oxidized or rancid cooking oil has polymerized into a cross-linked polymer that standard spotting cannot remove. We used potassium hydroxide, a strong alkaline solution, to break down the polymer chains through saponification and hydrolysis, converting them into soluble soaps that could wash away. The garment was thoroughly rinsed with distilled water to remove all alkaline residue, then neutralized with acetic acid to prevent yellowing.
homeSolutions: OxiClean or oxygen bleach soaked into the stain for 30 minutes, then rinsed — a weak oxidizing approach that may lighten very fresh oxidized oil slightly, but is unlikely to fully remove it.
Baking soda paste applied to the stain and left for 15 minutes, then brushed off and rinsed — very weak alkalinity, unlikely to help oxidized oil, but safe.

For extremely fresh oxidized oil (within hours), sometimes cold water rinse followed by repeated applications of mild liquid dishwashing detergent can prevent further oxidation, but will not remove polymerized oil.

meta.sourceRef: Expert Garment Restoration & Stain Removal Tips

products.name: Potassium Hydroxide (KOH, Caustic Potash, Lye)
products.note: Essential for oxidized oil saponification. HAZARDOUS — severe chemical burns risk. Heavy nitrile gloves and eye protection required. Use in ventilated area. Do not use on delicate fabrics. Professional treatment recommended.

products.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3% USP
products.note: For residual brown polymerized oil residue only. Safe on cotton.
